title: NGSS Science
description: A future-facing NGSS Grade 6 science course built around phenomena, virtual labs, evidence-based explanations, interactive practice, and student curiosity.

lessons.title: NGSS G6 Science 1: Ask Like a Scientist
lessons.description: Learn how scientists turn curiosity into testable questions and careful observations.

reviewAdvice.rewatchMessage: Rewatch the Ask Like a Scientist video and focus on how evidence supports explanations.

questions.prompt: In "Ask Like a Scientist", what should a scientist do first when investigating a new phenomenon?
questions.hint: Start with a question that can be tested with evidence.
questions.explanation: A strong investigation begins with a testable question.

encouragement.incorrect: Pause and think like a lab designer.

questions.prompt: In Ask Like a Scientist, a scientific claim is stronger when it is supported by ____.
questions.hint: Scientists use observations and data, not guesses.
questions.explanation: Evidence from observations, measurements, and patterns supports scientific claims.

encouragement.incorrect: Look for the word scientists use for data that supports a claim.

questions.prompt: True or false: A fair test changes one main variable at a time.
questions.hint: Changing too many things makes it hard to know what caused the result.
questions.explanation: A fair test isolates one independent variable so the result can be interpreted.

encouragement.incorrect: Think about what makes a test fair.

questions.prompt: Select the habits that make a strong NGSS science explanation.
questions.hint: A complete explanation connects claim, evidence, and reasoning.
questions.explanation: Strong explanations use evidence, patterns, and reasoning while keeping data visible.

encouragement.incorrect: Choose the habits that help another scientist trust the explanation.

questions.prompt: Put the Ask Like a Scientist investigation moves in the best order.
choices: Use evidence to explain the pattern
answer: Use evidence to explain the pattern
questions.hint: Start with what you observe, then test, measure, and explain.
questions.explanation: Scientific thinking moves from observation to question to data to evidence-based explanation.

encouragement.incorrect: Build the ladder from noticing to explaining.

questions.prompt: Which answer best shows that you can use the Ask Like a Scientist idea in a new situation?
choices: Explain a new example with the same model
Ignore the result if it is surprising
questions.answer: Explain a new example with the same model
questions.hint: Science sticks when you can use the model in a new situation.
questions.explanation: Applying the model to a new example shows deeper understanding.

encouragement.incorrect: Pick the choice that helps you use the idea again.

questions.prompt: When several observations in Ask Like a Scientist point the same way, scientists call that repeated result a ____.
questions.hint: Look for the word that means a repeated trend in data.
questions.explanation: Patterns help scientists make predictions and build explanations.

encouragement.incorrect: Think about repeated trends in the data.

questions.prompt: Which actions would make your Ask Like a Scientist notebook more useful?
choices: Erase measurements that do not fit
questions.hint: Good scientists keep useful details, especially surprises.
questions.explanation: Clear units, setup notes, and unexpected data help you review and improve an investigation.

encouragement.incorrect: Keep the choices that make the data easier to trust.

questions.prompt: After finishing Ask Like a Scientist, what is the strongest next investigation?
choices: Test one new variable and compare the data
Change everything and hope it works
questions.answer: Test one new variable and compare the data
questions.hint: A strong extension changes one thing and compares evidence.
questions.explanation: Testing one new variable lets you compare results and build a stronger explanation.

encouragement.correct: That is a real next-lab move.
encouragement.incorrect: Choose the move that creates better evidence.

questions.prompt: In 2-4 sentences, explain one idea from Ask Like a Scientist using evidence or a model. Then write one new question you would test next.
questions.hint: Use this pattern: I noticed... This suggests... Next I would test...
questions.explanation: Reflection saved. Strong scientists explain what they noticed and choose the next question.
questions.inputPlaceholder: I noticed... This suggests... Next I would test...

encouragement.incorrect: Add a little more detail: evidence, model, and next question.

lessons.title: NGSS G6 Science 2: Design a Fair Test
